speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 8, Albert Lichtmann. You were talking about the fate of your classmates.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when I came back, survived, I went back to schools to continue education. Out of the eight years of gymnasium, I still had to finish the seventh and eighth grade. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, in September of 1944, the school year didn't start. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By the way, our school building which I was so proud of, was expropriated by the Hungarian government during the war years, and actual education was in different buildings. But the building itself has a very important spot my memory and my heart. And years later, we had some reunion when had to school given back to us for one days after I was already an American citizen and we had a Budapest reunion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I went back to school from that 56 or so students, about 22 of us was only survived. More than half of the students of my class were no longer alive. And out of that 22, about four of them who were alive, didn't return to school, and didn't want to continue education at that level. And if we have time, I might mention the story of one of these classmates who didn't return to school, who was a close friend of mine. And we sat in the same bench. Whom I eventually, many years later, I recovered in the United States, where finally he finished education. But by then, we could not speak much Hungarian to each other. He married an American woman and he became an English teacher. And his Hungarian was very rusty. That was Thomas Weishaus. Both of his mother and father died in concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So back to my years at age 16, I had only one classmate whom I knew of, who also came back from concentration camp, , George Winkler who I remember well. And interestingly, just about two months ago, I knew he was alive. I met him then. But all these years went by, and I knew that he lives in Israel. But other classmates in Israel didn't know his whereabout. But apparently, he finally had some communication with one of those classmates in Israel who knew my address. And he was on his way visit somebody in Canada and the phone rang. And George Winkler says, if I tell you the name George Winkler, would you know who I am? He was talking to me in Hungarian. I said, yes, you were a classmate of mine. You and me are the only two I know of who came back from concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not only that, I remember you very well, because you were constantly involved with animation films, watching every cartoons. And you could imitate Donald Duck and the rest of those figures. And you entertained us constantly. He says, that's me. That's me. So only two of us came back from concentration camps. The other classmates survived either in the Budapest Ghetto, or were or hiding in different locations of the city, which was not an easy way to survive either, but that's the history. More than half of us was killed in different locations, different concentration camps. And I finished high school education in the Budapest Jewish Gymnasium. After that, I wanted to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somewhat emulate an American relative. My mother's first cousin lived in America and through my childhood I always heard, what a success her husband had in an industrial world. And that kind of a-- the childish hope and idealism. I thought that I will be some kind of an industrialist. And I entered, after I graduated from high school, I entered general mechanic, just the lowest level to learn how to deal with metal, and understand mechanics, and factory, and so on. And I worked in the Budapest telephone factory, which the name telephone factory doesn't give justice to the company's activities, because they were manufacturing movie projectors, railroads, railroad station systems, semaphores, and all these lighting systems, gas pumps and all kind of mechanically-advanced apparatuses. And I was there for a year. I received a diploma of a-- a-- a mechanic. It took me a year because, due to the fact that I went to higher education, this gymnasium degree, I didn't have to do the same thing as many people who were apprenticeship like myself, to learn the trade, had to go to school and their education lasted twice as long or more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I received a diploma of being a mechanic. But in the meantime, they were expropriating all the private ownership in the city and in the country, because Communism took strong foothold in the Hungarian political life. And Communists didn't permit private ownerships of factories, private ownerships of stores, private ownership of most anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: First it was only if you had more than 50 people working for you. Then it went down to practically if it was only a family business. Most all of those properties were taken over by the-- by the government. And that's how my father, again, lost his business. And his private business, which was again, the hat and wholesale, which developed very nicely after World War II, was the expropriated. He just one day was told to get out of the store. And he went to his cash registers, and including that. Whatever is cash in the register is not yours anymore. And they just made him leave with all his inventory left behind. And that was, this time the Communists who took over his livelihood. Father then got a job and worked in the same trade in a department store, selling hats, on one on one, not wholesale. And not for himself, but for the government-owned store. And in the meantime, I changed my mind about mechanics and out of the two higher-education universities-- which I got accepted both to the engineering university, engineering means licensed high graduate of university education for three, four years. I didn't enter that. And I entered the medical university to the amazement of the director of the factory, who wanted to offer some scholarship for me and go and stay with mechanics. And I just said, it's medicine this time. I'm-- I'm not going to stay with mechanics.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] why did I decide on medicine? You know, sometime they ask questions. I cannot really explain it so well. It wasn't that I thought that I were or I jokingly say that my grandmother already noticed that I want to help [? on ?] people, because when she cut her finger, I already knew how to attend and put a little pressure on it or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I was interested maybe in physiology and curiosity for the functioning of the human body. But somehow, between the two, I chose medicine. And I graduated in Budapest. And I practiced medicine for three years in Hungary. And then, irony of life, that's while the Russians freed me, and I am very thankful that I happened to be on their way to beat the Third Reich, and they gave back my life and freedom. But on the other hand, the system during Communism has somewhat took away my enthusiasm to live under the system forever. And when the revolution broke out, I escaped from the country, from Hungary, and-- and crossed the border over to Austria. And then I have made contact with different relief organizations, as well as some relatives in the United States. And in a few months, two months exactly, my time was two months to live in Vienna when I was cleared all the papers to enter the United States as an immigrant. I left my parents behind. It was very difficult for me, because again, we were separated, not knowing that the Iron Curtain will eventually lift and how do we see each other again. And I have entered the United States. I had difficulty with the English language. I had some high school English, but it was far from being conversational.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the significance of the day you arrived in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a rebirth. It was a rebirth because I chose this country. And I arrived here on my own choice to be in this country. It happens to be so that I arrived on my birthday, on February the 11th. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I was very interested in seeing America. But I was stationed in Camp Kilmer, which is in New Jersey. And that's a location where the American military forces were gathered before they went over to Europe on the Liberty ships to go to Europe, transports. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was in Camp Kilmer for a while with many other Hungarians who were waiting for the clearing process, and health, and political alliances, and so on, which-- which eventually resulted that my papers were approved and given to me as a-- as a newcomer, a-- a-- a legal immigrant to the United States of America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Interestingly also, I contacted some distant relatives of ours who lived in New York. How did I know about them? That was-- was one of the most interesting coincidence, again. As a physician back in Hungary, practicing obstetrics and gynecology on my own by then and having a patient whom I operated on in an emergency situation, went to visit her a couple of days after the operation on a daily visit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the room, there was a lady who looked very well-dressed, sitting at her bed. And she introduced herself in Hungarian that this is my daughter. I came to visit her from London, hearing about that she had this emergency surgery. And you are the doctor. I'd like to thank taking care of her. And I was very happy to see that she is doing well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the mother is happy seeing her daughter from England. And I said, you live in England, in London? Yes. I said, you know, that sounded like to us, like another planet. Because in Hungary those days, nobody crossed the border anywhere. That was a very strong Iron Curtain, not only in its meaning of education and-- and-- and correspondence between one side or the other of the Iron Curtain.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were not tourists all over in Western Europe. You couldn't leave the country. And no country I seen beside Hungary, at that point, except during my little excursion into the concentration camp on the Austrian border. So I was very interested about the west. And I said to this lady, says, the story tells, my mother tells that we have some relatives who survived World War II. They are German part of the family. And somebody lives in London. Do you mind if I ask you, when you go back to London, pick up the phone book and see you if there is a Schleyer in the book. She says, I sure will do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So time goes by. The revolution breaks out in Hungary. And in the confusion of a revolution and of these days, mail is not daily delivered, but somehow, some mail is delivered. And what comes on the mail, a letter from London. And this relative, distant relative, second cousin of my mother, an elderly man, who lives in London by the name of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I forgot this, only the first name.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow, writes to me and writes in German that he's very happy to hear that my mother, and I, and my father survived World War II. And in this terrible time of-- of the revolution in Hungary, which they know about, he would like to see that if I ever get out of the country, or visiting, or whatever, he would like to meet me and let them know. And it was an unusual letter [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and an interesting coincidence. So it comes the day that I decide to escape from Hungary. And I don't want to go into details that escaping was crossing the border on foot. The border which I knew from concentration camp, I am crossing it now the other direction. The Russians freed me. I am ironically leaving the Russian-dominated Hungary to go to the west. And I arrive. And successfully crossing the border, not an easy task, and arrived to Vienna, where I start to make contacts with people whom I wanted to try to get contact with. I had a little address book with me and the physician handbag. And that was my all, my positions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I contacted in Vienna some international relief organizations, including a Jewish relief organization. And they were already having thousands and thousands of Hungarian refugees crossing the border. And I contacted a young boy who my mother talked a lot about, who she met in Sweden when the Swedish Red Cross took her to Sweden.

speaker: interviewee
text: This boy came with a school group to cheer them up and entertain them. And was a very personable young boy, who just kept returning to this group of Hungarian Jewish Holocaust survivors, and apparently learned Hungarian language from them to the point that my mother said he spoke unbelievably well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, why do I tell about this? Because it just happens to be so that I met him in the United States here later, when he took residence in America. And when my parents joined me in the United States, my mother had a chance to see him again. And he was like, she was like a mother to him as well, because this was a fantastic story that this boy seen mother when she was just a barely-alive Holocaust survivor in Sweden. And-- and that's a side part of the story. So I did contact relatives, including a name who this Schleyer in London gave to me, I wrote in London from Vienna. I had difficulty how to communicate. I had people to translate my letter. I don't-- didn't speak German well enough to write letters. I didn't speak English well enough. So I always had some intermediary who helped me with my letter and contacting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this Mr. Schleyer, gave me George Schleyer's name in New York, his brother, who also relative to my mother. And when I arrived to New York after being released from Camp Kilmer, I contacted. Actually, I contacted them from Camp Kilmer, and he and another relative of mine, whom I didn't know about, but he knew about in New York-- again, second cousins or so-- were that couple and the older couple of the Schleyers, who he was in his late 70s, came to Camp Kilmer to visit me. We couldn't converse, because they didn't speak a word of Hungarian. They were the German side of the family. And my English was absolutely minimal. So we had some, again, some interpreter who helped us. And I keep up in knowing them and keep in touch, not only with the Manns-- M-A-N-N-- Dieter Mann and his wife, because the Schleyer were too old and they passed away. And-- and I met also this cousin of my mother, Mrs. Polachek, who is an interesting story of her own. They were very successful people in the United States and we always hoped that someday we will see them and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell about you marriage.

speaker: interviewee
text: My marriage, a very happy one-- a very happy one. My wife and I married 34 years. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was, as they say, [? bashert. ?] [? Bashert ?] is an expression, Yiddish German that it was meant in Heaven. How can you be so schmaltzy, to talk about a marriage like this? Well, what led up to the marriage, it was too, very coincidental. And I am not going to repeat the story. My family, my children, and my wife knows it. We got married 34 years ago. We dated before we got engaged, we seen each other no longer, no more than four or five weekends. She lived in New York. I lived in Washington. And I went to New York several times, four or five times. And by then, I asked if-- I told her I have serious intentions. I would like to marry her. She said she thinks about it. And then I received a telegram from her, and there was one simple word on it. It said igen in Hungarian, which means yes. She is Hungarian by birth, but I met her in the United States. And I knew about-- I knew about-- I didn't know her, but I knew her uncle, who was just one year ahead of me in high school. And that's how, when he visited in the United States at the-- at the World Federation of Hungarian Jewish event, I bumped into him and his-- I recognized him. And then finally, he recognized me after many years being in the same high school. He talked to me about his visit here from Morocco and that led to my getting to know about this lovely girl, who was love at first sight. And we are married 34 years. And we have two daughters. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Judith. Her maiden name is Judith Bardos, B-A-R-D-O-S. And her father and her mother survived the Holocaust. My father-in-law is alive. He is reaching, as a matter of fact, he is 88 years old today. He came into the United States a little later than I did, in 1958. I came in 1957. I arrived here on February 11, 1957. And he was an assistant chief of internal medicine in a well-known Budapest hospital, [INAUDIBLE]. And he repeated taking examination and becoming physician like myself in the United States. And he practiced in New York for many, many years. He gave up his medical practice about eight years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the names and ages of your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My children names and ages. I am always have to think for a moment when I have to quote names. My younger daughter was born in 1965. My older daughter was born in 1963. And-- and they are married. The husbands are [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very wonderful two gentleman, whom we love very much. My order girl has two children, a boy Samantha, who is two years and three months old, and a young one who is 3 and 1/2 months old, a boy, Andrew. They like to call him Drew. And my younger daughter has a year and three months old daughter, Julia. And we love them dearly. We are very lucky that they're living in the area. It wasn't like that when they first got married, but eventually, they settled in the Washington area. And we love every moment of it. And this is the happiest part of my interview, to say that I am extremely lucky. I am very thankful to God. I am very thankful to-- to all what led to me to achieve a successful, wonderful life in the United States. I hope I will have good health for many years to come, because I am one of the luckiest Holocaust survivor. I never talked much about my experiences until this interview. My family knows tidbits about it. And there is not everything one can tell in a few hours. But I try to summarize it as much as possible. The feelings of being humiliated for your religion, for your race, I was Hungarian. My race, I thought it was just a different religion I had, but I was very Hungarian. I loved every bit of it. Just like many of the Hungarian Jews, we sort of wanted to be Hungarians. And so it was for German Jews. They were more German from what I understand than-- than many of the German so-called [? aria, ?] which means the chosen race of the upper, the-- the top race. The Germans thought that there is nobody like them. And I just would like to say that it cannot be erased from one's memory, never, what I went through. But as-- as years go by, I opened up a little more to talk about it. And on the urging of my daughters and my wife, I agreed to give this interview. I-- I would see that I was controlling my emotions as much as I could. I worked in hospital for 35 years where few people hardly knew about all of this. I never talked much about my concentration camp experiences or the Jewish experience of growing up in Hungary. Because I wanted them to look at me as a-- an individual whom they don't feel pity or sorry for, that part of his life was so horrible and terrible. And-- and I kept very silent about it. And I think that's how I wanted it to be. On the other hand, I am now 68 years old. I was 16 at the time when all these things took place. And in another 10, 15 years there will be not too many of us who are alive and well to tell about it, and not many of us who were old enough, like 16, who are alive and well to tell about it. Because if somebody was four, five years younger, 10 years, eight years old, they-- they lived it through. The parents were trying to hide them or wherever they survived. But they don't have a-- a firsthand information, knowledge of seeing Nazism, hatred at its best. Because what the Holocaust did for us Jews, is, it has it's, a very, very major segment of darkness in the history of the world. And I hope and I try to do everything in my life, which literally I can, to speak how much hatred by education should be eliminated. It is one of the strongest force. Hatred is easier to spread than love. And people find every occasion to hate. Many people do. And some people take advantage of that. There are some leaders who thrive on that and then all kind of horrible things-- ethnic cleansing, Holocausts, and tragedies for humankind can occur, which we would like very much to avoid, and should never, ever happen again. And if there is anything to learn and-- and-- and testify, it is that this was a time in my life and-- and in the history of humankind which should be a lesson how not to behave to each other, and how not to communicate with each other, and how not to hate each other. And many testimonies like mine and the Holocaust Museum here in Washington will give a wonderful educational opportunity, and the Shoah program which I am participating here today. And if I get this copy of this taping, maybe in years to come, when my children, grandchildren, and the following generations can read, and learn, and hear about it, it will h have some educational value. And I thank you for your time. And it was emotional for me at times. And I'm sure I will probably think of it that maybe at times, I should have said more, different, more eloquent. But all of it is done in memory of my relatives I lost and classmates I lost from the Budapest high school, because they were-- they were my life. And thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
